_comment: Decision ledger for the ACTIVE line. Each Friday the daily routine appends one rebalance with target weights (ticker -> fraction; the remainder is held as cash). Self-financing: no new money in/out. Weights are applied at that date's close and held until the next entry. Never edit past entries. See CLAUDE.md.

rebalances.label: Inception
rebalances.rationale: Start equal-weight: 1/15 in every name. Identical to the buy & hold book on day one.

rebalances.label: Trim the parabola, fund the laggards
rebalances.rationale: Six months in, the memory/storage trade has gone vertical: Seagate (+224%), Marvell (+213%), Micron (+211%) and SK Hynix (+203%) have each roughly tripled YTD and now carry far more weight — and risk — than I want in four correlated DRAM/HBM names. I'm trimming all four to ~4.5% and recycling the proceeds into quality laggards that haven't run: Broadcom (+10%), Arista (+22%), TSMC (+33%) and Equinix (+40%), plus a contrarian add to the only loser in the book, Constellation Energy (-31%). I'm also parking ~3.5% in cash as dry powder after a move this parabolic. Conviction tilt, not a wholesale reshuffle — all 15 names stay in.

rebalances.label: Partly re-add memory after Micron's blowout; keep Broadcom on Jalapeño
rebalances.rationale: Two weeks ago I trimmed the memory/HBM names hard, and the thesis has only gotten stronger, so I'm partially re-adding. On June 24 Micron reported a record quarter and guided FQ4 to ~$50B revenue, ~86% gross margin and ~$31 EPS, with HBM sold out through 2026 and HBM4 ramping twice as fast as HBM3E; SK Hynix is similarly sold out, and DRAM ASPs are inflecting up sharply as capacity tilts to HBM. I'm lifting MU to 7.5% and SK Hynix to 6.5% (the cleanest sold-out plays), with MRVL to 6.0% and STX to 5.0% — still well below where they'd sit on price alone, but no longer fighting a confirmed structural shortage. Funding comes from shaving the stretched equipment/data-centre winners (ASML, EQIX, TSM, ANET each to ~6.5-7.0%) and from cash, which drops to ~5.5%. I'm keeping Broadcom as the single biggest weight at 8.5% after the June 24 OpenAI 'Jalapeño' custom-inference-chip reveal and reiterated >$100B AI-semi guidance — it's still only +9% YTD, the cheapest growth in the book. CEG held at 7.0%: it signed a Walmart nuclear PPA and screens cheap vs a ~$360 median target, but it's been the lone loser two weeks running so I'm not adding more. All 15 names stay in; weights sum to 0.945 with the rest in cash.

rebalances.label: Hold the memory line, keep Broadcom on top, add to the CEG laggard
rebalances.rationale: July-4 observed holiday means markets are shut Friday, so I'm dating this to Thursday 2026-07-02. The book is now +90.7% active vs the S&P's +9.1%, and the memory/HBM thesis has if anything strengthened: on July 2 TrendForce reported SK Hynix is removing the price cap in its long-term agreements so contract prices can fully track a tightening spot market (Micron kept a cap at Q2-2026 levels), and server-DRAM demand is guided up ~60% into 2027. But MU (+209%), SK Hynix (+201%), STX (+186%) and MRVL (+175%) are already the most stretched names in the book, and a ~June-25 antitrust suit alleging a coordinated HBM-driven DDR3/DDR4 curtailment adds headline/legal risk — so I'm holding these four steady at last week's 7.5/6.5/5.0/6.0 rather than chasing them higher: participate in the shortage, don't press it. Broadcom stays the single largest weight at 8.5%: after the June-24 OpenAI 'Jalapeño' reveal (1.3GW in 2027, part of a 10GW deal through 2029) it has actually cheapened to just +4% YTD — still the cheapest growth in the book. The one change of conviction is Constellation: it's the lone loser at -34.5% after a June $3.1B secondary, but the nuclear-for-AI story is intact (reaffirmed $11-12 EPS, ~$360 median target), so I'm lifting CEG from 7.0% to 7.5% funded by trimming stretched winner AMAT (+125%) from 6.0% to 5.5%. Equipment (TSMC $52-56B capex, ASML raised + €12B buyback, AMAT equipment +30%) and networking (Arista FY26 ~$11.5B) all remain healthy but already-run, so I leave them put. All 15 names stay in; weights sum to 0.945 with ~5.5% cash.

rebalances.label: Lean into Broadcom's Apple-through-2031 catalyst; hold the memory line steady
rebalances.rationale: The active book is now +99.0% vs the S&P's +10.0%. This week's cleanest new catalyst is Broadcom: on July 9 it extended its custom-silicon partnership with Apple through 2031 (~20% of AVGO revenue) and guided next-quarter AI-chip revenue to ~$16B, up >200% YoY, on hyperscaler design wins across Google, Meta and OpenAI, reaffirming its path to >$100B AI-semi revenue by 2027 — yet AVGO is still the cheapest name in the book at only +15.8% YTD. I'm lifting AVGO from 8.5% to 9.5% to press the single highest-conviction, lowest-multiple name. The memory/HBM shortage thesis is if anything stronger (SK Hynix scrapped long-term price caps, Samsung is pushing another ~20% Q3 DRAM hike, and Jefferies sees DRAM +40-50% in Q3 and +30-40% in Q4 with no relief before 2028), but MU (+214.6%), SK Hynix (+210.7%), STX (+210.3%) and MRVL (+172.5%) are already the four most stretched names in the book and still carry the June antitrust/price-fixing suit as headline risk — so I participate but don't chase: I hold MU/SK Hynix/MRVL at 7.5/6.5/6.0 and shave the most extended, lowest-quality memory name, Seagate, from 5.0% to 4.0% to fund the Broadcom add. CEG stays 7.5% as the contrarian value anchor — still the lone loser at -31.3%, but the nuclear-for-AI story is intact (Walmart + Microsoft PPAs, $11-12 EPS reaffirmed, ~1GW of uprates). Equipment (TSMC raising 2026 capex to $52-56B, ASML at record highs) and networking remain healthy but already-run, so I leave them put. All 15 names stay in; weights sum to 0.945 with ~5.5% cash.
